Study in Germany 🇩🇪 the education system in Germany is free. By that it is meant that you do not have to pay tuition fees, apart for the semester ticket which also serves as a public transportation ticket there is the possibility to make your studies in English, both Bachelor's and Master's. A tip here:when you head over to DAAD webpage, filter out study programmes based on the desired study language despite of the free education, there is the chance to receive a . Depending on your (extra) curricular performance, you can apply for various public and / or private organizations offered scholarships. On you can again look up for a suitable scholarship the living expenses in Germany are on average not higher than in other European countries. As a student you can save some with the offered student discounts, buying groceries in less expensive supermarkets and share a flat with a fellow colleague in Germany it is welcomed and common that students work part - time beside their studies. It may be a practical paid apprenticeship next to the study or a student job elsewhere. That provides some and some work experience , at the same time

Besides University studies (higher education), there is in Germany the option to do an . What does it stand for? This is a Vocational training distinguished in: Dual vocational training ("Duale Berufsausbildung") - typically taught at a Berufsschule, where you learn and work at the same time. (duration: 2-3.5 years) School-based vocational training ("Schulische Ausbildung") - school based training provided at "Berufsfachschule" with practical on-the-job training, but only as an additional option, hence there js no ongoing salary paid Dual vocational degree ("Ausbildungsorientiertes duales Studium") - this Dual degree is taught by Universities or "Berufsakademie". It requires German University entrance qualification (equivalent to completion of Abitur). Students receive a certified degree and on-the job-training/work experience (limited to 2 years) certificate. A trainee level salary is paid during training period Based on your future plans and aspirations, you can opt for a vocational training which brings your foot on the job market, by learning and working at the same time

Art doesn't just ask to be seen - it asks to be felt. ️ Learning beyond the classroom is at the heart of SRH's CORE principle - and the MA Strategic Design program at SRH Campus Berlin lives this fully. Our students dived into the spaces where culture, critique, and creativity converge: Berlin's world-class museums and galleries From the raw, labyrinthine worlds of Diane Arbus and the political choreography of Ligia Lewis at the , to the bold cinematic vision of Helmut Newton and the quietly powerful storytelling of fashion photography at the - our students experienced art that challenges, unsettles, and inspires in equal measure Led by Prof. Jasper Halfmann and shaped by the vision of program directors Prof. Katrin Androschin and Prof. Julia Leihener, these visits are an integral part of how we train designers to think critically: How does space shape feeling? How does structure influence meaning? How does a single image carry culture, critique, and emotion all at once? Because designers who learn to see deeper, create with greater intention.

Since 2020, the SOS - Songs of Substance praxis course at SRH has brought The Role of Music in Society into the heart of the conversation. SOS creates space for dialogue on music, awareness, and societal change It's inspiring to see a project born here at SRH evolve into a major event beyond our campus We're excited for keynotes and musical performances from our SRH music lecturer Jocelyn B. Smith (SOS founder), Volker Holly Schlott, and Kai Brückner on 10th November at the House of Music Berlin

studifygermany 2w How to Choose a German City: Budget vs. Career Most students pick Berlin or Munich because everyone talks about them. Then they struggle with: €1,500/month rent Impossible accommodation searches Brutal job competition Here's the reality most advisors won't tell you: You don't have to study in Berlin. The smart strategy is choosing by YOUR goals, not Instagram hype. TIER 1: HYPE CITIES (Berlin, Munich, Frankfurt, Hamburg) Living: €1,000-1,500/month Jobs: More available Community: Very international Good if: You have strong finances or family support Bad if: Budget is tight TIER 2: GROWING CITIES (Stuttgart, Cologne, Hannover, Düsseldorf) Living: €800-1,000/month Jobs: Good market (industry-dependent) Community: Smaller but authentic Good if: You want balance between cost and opportunity Bad if: You need big international scene TIER 3: AFFORDABLE CITIES (Leipzig, Dresden, Aachen) Living: €600-800/month Jobs: Limited Community: Great student life Good if: Budget is your priority Bad if: You need career opportunities immediately after THE REAL STRATEGY: 1. Identify your industry: - Tech? Berlin - Automotive? Stuttgart - Finance? Frankfurt - Engineering? Munich 2. Calculate true cost: Living cost + Part-time income potential = Real expense 3. Remember: You can move after graduation. You don't need to stay in your study city. Choose city for budget + studies now. Move for career later. studifygermany 4w Your Documents Decide Your Admission. Period. Most students think their GPA decides if they get into a German university. They're wrong. Here's what actually happens: GPA gets you in the "maybe" pile. Your documents get you into "yes" or "no." We've reviewed 500+ applications. The pattern is clear: Students with 3.2 GPA + strong SOP ️ Accepted Students with 3.8 GPA + generic SOP Rejected This happens more often than you'd think. Here's what universities actually evaluate: 1. SOP (does your narrative make sense?) 2. LOR (does a professor vouch for you?) 3. Work Experience (what have you actually done?) 4. CV/Portfolio (can you prove it?) 5. Language Certificate (can you handle studies?) 6. Transcript (are there explanations for gaps?) Notice what's NOT on this list? Your actual GPA number. Why? Because context matters more than numbers. A 2.4 GPA with 2 years of relevant work experience beats a 3.8 GPA with zero professional proof. The students we help succeed understand this. They invest in document quality. Not just corrections. Strategic reframing. The SOP becomes a narrative that connects your past to your future. The CV becomes proof of your capabilities. The work letter becomes validation of your potential. That's when admissions happen. Introducing Prof. Dr. Varun Gupta, a distinguished academic here at Gisma University of Applied Sciences and Head of the Multidisciplinary Research Centre for Innovations in SMEs (MrciS). With a rich academic background, he has held key roles at institutions like Universidad de Alcalá, Lund University, and several others across Europe. Featuring a wealth of professional experience in software engineering, innovation management, and globalization, Prof. Gupta holds multiple advanced degrees, including a Doctorate (cum laude) in Organizational Engineering and Computer Science, and an MBA. He serves as an Associate Editor for high-impact journals such as IEEE Access and PLOS One and has led numerous funded projects, including an €18K initiative on Global Business Model Innovation. Prof. Gupta has authored over 16 books and mentored numerous doctoral and master's students. Tap bio to learn more about Prof. Gupta's expertise.
